text: Hypertension, or high blood pressure, is defined as a systolic blood pressure of 130 mmHg or higher, or diastolic blood pressure of 80 mmHg or higher. It is a major risk factor for heart attack, stroke, kidney failure, and other cardiovascular diseases. Lifestyle modifications such as reducing sodium intake, increasing physical activity, maintaining a healthy weight, limiting alcohol, and quitting smoking are first-line recommendations.

First-line antihypertensive drug classes include ACE inhibitors (e.g. lisinopril), ARBs (e.g. losartan), thiazide diuretics (e.g. hydrochlorothiazide), and calcium channel blockers (e.g. amlodipine). The choice depends on patient comorbidities: ACE inhibitors or ARBs are preferred in patients with diabetes or chronic kidney disease; thiazides are often used in uncomplicated hypertension.

Type 2 diabetes mellitus is characterised by insulin resistance and progressive beta-cell dysfunction. Diagnostic criteria include fasting plasma glucose ≥126 mg/dL, 2-hour plasma glucose ≥200 mg/dL during an oral glucose tolerance test, HbA1c ≥6.5%, or random plasma glucose ≥200 mg/dL with symptoms. Lifestyle intervention remains the cornerstone of management.

Metformin is the preferred initial pharmacologic agent for type 2 diabetes due to its efficacy, safety profile, low cost, and possible cardiovascular benefits. GLP-1 receptor agonists (e.g. semaglutide, liraglutide) and SGLT-2 inhibitors (e.g. empagliflozin, dapagliflozin) are recommended add-ons for patients with established cardiovascular disease or high cardiovascular risk.

Asthma is a chronic inflammatory airway disease characterised by variable and reversible airflow obstruction, bronchial hyperresponsiveness, and airway inflammation. Common triggers include allergens, exercise, cold air, respiratory infections, and irritants like tobacco smoke. Spirometry showing at least 12% and 200 mL improvement in FEV1 after bronchodilator confirms reversible airflow obstruction.

Asthma management follows a stepwise approach. Short-acting beta-agonists (SABAs, e.g. salbutamol/albuterol) are used for acute symptom relief. Inhaled corticosteroids (ICS) are the mainstay of long-term control. Long-acting beta-agonists (LABAs) are added at step 3 in combination with ICS. Patients should be assessed at every visit for adherence, inhaler technique, and trigger avoidance.

Fever is defined as a core body temperature above 38°C (100.4°F). It is typically a physiological response to infection, inflammation, or immune activation. Common causes include viral upper respiratory infections, influenza, urinary tract infections, and pneumonia. Persistent fever lasting more than 3 weeks without an identified cause is classified as fever of unknown origin (FUO).

Antipyretic agents used to manage fever include paracetamol (acetaminophen) and NSAIDs such as ibuprofen. Paracetamol is preferred in children and patients with gastrointestinal issues. NSAIDs should be avoided in patients with renal impairment, peptic ulcer disease, or those taking anticoagulants. Fever reduction does not treat the underlying cause and should be considered alongside diagnostic evaluation.

Dyslipidaemia refers to abnormal levels of lipids in the blood, including elevated LDL cholesterol, reduced HDL cholesterol, or elevated triglycerides. The primary goal of lipid management is reducing LDL-C to reduce cardiovascular risk. Statins are the first-line pharmacological treatment; high-intensity statins such as atorvastatin 40-80mg or rosuvastatin 20-40mg are recommended for high-risk patients.

Major Depressive Disorder (MDD) is characterised by persistent low mood or loss of interest lasting at least two weeks, accompanied by symptoms such as sleep disturbance, fatigue, psychomotor changes, difficulty concentrating, feelings of worthlessness, and recurrent thoughts of death. First-line treatments include selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) such as sertraline or escitalopram, combined with cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT).

Antibiotic stewardship is critical to combating antimicrobial resistance. Antibiotics are ineffective against viral infections such as the common cold or influenza and should not be prescribed for them. Common bacterial infections requiring antibiotics include streptococcal pharyngitis (penicillin or amoxicillin), uncomplicated urinary tract infections (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole or nitrofurantoin), and community-acquired pneumonia (amoxicillin or azithromycin for outpatients).
source: Infectious Disease and Antimicrobial Stewardship Reference

text: The WHO analgesic ladder provides a stepwise approach to pain management: Step 1 uses non-opioid analgesics (paracetamol, NSAIDs) for mild pain; Step 2 adds weak opioids (codeine, tramadol) for moderate pain; Step 3 uses strong opioids (morphine, oxycodone) for severe pain. Adjuvant medications such as antidepressants and anticonvulsants are used at any step for neuropathic pain.

A balanced diet for adult health should include adequate intake of fruits, vegetables, whole grains, lean proteins, and healthy fats. The Dietary Guidelines recommend at least 5 portions of fruit and vegetables per day, limiting saturated fats to less than 10% of total energy, limiting free sugars to less than 5% of total energy, and consuming at least 25-30g of dietary fibre daily. Processed and ultra-processed foods should be minimised.

Adequate sleep is essential for physical and mental health. Adults require 7-9 hours of sleep per night. Chronic sleep deprivation is associated with increased risk of obesity, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, impaired immune function, and mental health disorders. Sleep hygiene practices include maintaining a consistent sleep schedule, limiting screen time before bed, avoiding caffeine in the evening, and keeping the bedroom cool and dark.

Vaccination is one of the most cost-effective public health interventions. Core adult vaccines include annual influenza vaccine, COVID-19 vaccine (primary series plus boosters), tetanus-diphtheria-pertussis (Tdap) every 10 years, pneumococcal vaccine for adults 65+, and shingles vaccine (Shingrix) for adults 50+. Immunocompromised individuals may require modified schedules and should avoid live attenuated vaccines.
